So I've made a thing that searches the existing abstract numbering schemes for the style of the current paragraph, and sets a reasonable abstract style based on that if possible:

```
def list_number(doc, par, prev=None, level=None, num=True):
    """
    Makes a paragraph into a list item with a specific level and
    optional restart.

    An attempt will be made to retreive an abstract numbering style that
    corresponds to the style of the paragraph. If that is not possible,
    the default numbering or bullet style will be used based on the
    ``num`` parameter.

    Parameters
    ----------
    doc : docx.document.Document
        The document to add the list into.
    par : docx.paragraph.Paragraph
        The paragraph to turn into a list item.
    prev : docx.paragraph.Paragraph or None
        The previous paragraph in the list. If specified, the numbering
        and styles will be taken as a continuation of this paragraph.
        If omitted, a new numbering scheme will be started.
    level : int or None
        The level of the paragraph within the outline. If ``prev`` is
        set, defaults to the same level as in ``prev``. Otherwise,
        defaults to zero.
    num : bool
        If ``prev`` is :py:obj:`None` and the style of the paragraph
        does not correspond to an existing numbering style, this will
        determine wether or not the list will be numbered or bulleted.
        The result is not guaranteed, but is fairly safe for most Word
        templates.
    """
    xpath_options = {
        True: {'single': 'count(w:lvl)=1 and ', 'level': 0},
        False: {'single': '', 'level': level},
    }

    def style_xpath(prefer_single=True):
        """
        The style comes from the outer-scope variable ``par.style.name``.
        """
        style = par.style.name
        return (
            'w:abstractNum['
                '{single}w:lvl[@w:ilvl="{level}"]/w:pStyle[@w:val="{style}"]'
            ']/@w:abstractNumId'
        ).format(style=style, **xpath_options[prefer_single])

    def type_xpath(prefer_single=True):
        """
        The type is from the outer-scope variable ``num``.
        """
        type = 'decimal' if num else 'bullet'
        return (
            'w:abstractNum['
                '{single}w:lvl[@w:ilvl="{level}"]/w:numFmt[@w:val="{type}"]'
            ']/@w:abstractNumId'
        ).format(type=type, **xpath_options[prefer_single])

    def get_abstract_id():
        """
        Select as follows:

            1. Match single-level by style (get min ID)
            2. Match exact style and level (get min ID)
            3. Match single-level decimal/bullet types (get min ID)
            4. Match decimal/bullet in requested level (get min ID)
            3. 0
        """
        for fn in (style_xpath, type_xpath):
            for prefer_single in (True, False):
                xpath = fn(prefer_single)
                ids = numbering.xpath(xpath)
                if ids:
                    return min(int(x) for x in ids)
        return 0

    if (prev is None or
            prev._p.pPr is None or
            prev._p.pPr.numPr is None or
            prev._p.pPr.numPr.numId is None):
        numbering = doc.part.numbering_part.numbering_definitions._numbering
        # Compute the abstract ID first by style, then by num
        anum = get_abstract_id()
        # Set the concrete numbering based on the abstract numbering ID
        num = numbering.add_num(anum)
        # Make sure to override the abstract continuation property
        num.add_lvlOverride(ilvl=0).add_startOverride(1)
        # Extract the newly-allocated concrete numbering ID
        num = num.numId
        if level is None:
            level = 0
    else:
        # Get the previous concrete numbering ID
        num = prev._p.pPr.numPr.numId.val
        if level is None:
            level = prev._p.pPr.numPr.ilvl.val
    par._p.get_or_add_pPr().get_or_add_numPr().get_or_add_numId().val = num
    par._p.get_or_add_pPr().get_or_add_numPr().get_or_add_ilvl().val = level
```

This is in no way comprehensive or particularly robust, but it works pretty well for what I am trying to do.

> Each of the categorical variable only contains 10-20 values, whereas the universe might contain 50-100 values. Therefore I need to create a dummy variable as a placeholder for those values for future predictions.

You need to re-train your XGBoost or RF model periodically, as more data becomes available. Today's model does not contain "prediction logic" for handling future category levels, so it would be making non-sensical/bad predictions in that case.

> does that mean there is no way to add those zero dummy fields in the xgb conversion?

A categorical feature is defined by a single `/PMML/DataDictionary/DataField` element. So, at the moment it contains 10-20 category levels (each represented as a `DataField/Value` element). If you input an unseen category level during model scoring, then by default the model will fail with an exception message "category level $value is not defined for field $x". However, you can "relax" this behaviour by chaning the value of the corresponding `MiningSchema/MiningField@invalidValueTreatment` attribute from `returnInvalid` to `asIs`: http://dmg.org/pmml/v4-3/MiningSchema.html#xsdType_INVALID-VALUE-TREATMENT-METHOD

You can specify the "invalid value treatment method" for a column by prepending `sklearn2pmml.CategoricalDomain` to the list of its transformations:
```Python
mapper = DataFrameMapper([
  ("x", [CategoricalDomain(invalid_value_treatment = "as_is"), LabelBinarizer()])
])
```

Also see this thread: https://groups.google.com/d/topic/jpmml/g01KjriBlcs/discussion

Nope, not at the moment, since I don't use probablepeople. But if I were using it in my projects, I could imagine the following scenarios coming up at some point:

1. Hmm, I think I'm hitting a bug in version X of probablepeople. Can I easily find the code the corresponds to version X so I can investigate further?
2. I noticed a change in behavior in my application sometime after I upgraded from version X of probablepeople to version Y. I'm not sure if my project is culpable, or if it's probablepeople. Can I easily diff the two versions of probablepeople to see if that yields any clues?
3. A known bug in probablepeople got fixed recently. Did the fix get released so I can upgrade my dependency accordingly? What version do I need to upgrade to?

In projects following common git/GitHub conventions (at least, from what I know), all these scenarios would be easy to navigate. Each release would be tagged in git, which means the version is navigable and diffable against other versions in GitHub. And each tagged release in git would have a corresponding release on PyPI.

Right now there is no way for people to understand probablepeople's behavior at arbitrary releases. Generally this is fine, since most people aren't going to need to do that. This kind of stuff does come up though when people need to track issues with their upstream dependencies, or when as a maintainer you are trying to correlate user reports with code changes.

It'll also be confusing to people that the tagged release on GitHub (0.5.3) is newer than the latest release on PyPI (0.5.2). I would wager that most would assume, like I did, that that is a mistake. Why would something be tagged with a release version but not be published to PyPI? There can perhaps be a good reason for that, but it won't be obvious to the typical open source user or contributor.

I hope this clarifies what prompted me to open this issue.

Since this is so clearly discouraged, I'll try removing it and see what happens. Then we can add encoding and decoding fixes where they are needed - to have an explicit handling of wrong encodings and possibly track them down to their source.

IMO the only way to deal with encoding issues is at the source, the possible issues that are mentioned in [the StackOverflow answer](https://stackoverflow.com/a/3828742/405682) do not sound nice to try and track down... especially in some distant future where maintaining Python 2.7 support is a terrible burden to someone.

I suggest:

1) We isolate all Python 2.7 compatibility handling to a central location (I've suggested this before) - in this example, there's some compat code sitting in the middle of module load time.

2) We try dealing with terminal encoding by having utility functions for all terminal invocation - this will also make it easier to mock stuff for testing purposes

3) All encoding issues from data (CC server etc) should be dealt with at the source. Any problems that occur in this regard are IMO fine, we just need to fix them, when we see them. Since Python 3 has UTF-8 encoding and decoding built into a lot of functions, we won't see these issues in anything but Python 2.7 -- however, if we do see them, we would know that some data we are using was wrongly encoded and has a danger of getting wrongly interpreted at some point.

Okay, so the reason it fails is because MISP locks this event as it doesn't comes from a synchronisation.

The way to solve this issue is to **create the event** with the `locked` field set to `True`. 

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3                                                                                                               
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-                                                                                                              
                                                                                                                                     
from pymisp import PyMISP                                                                                                            
from keys import misp_url, misp_key                                                                                                  
                                                                                                                                     
from pymisp import MISPEvent, MISPOrganisation                                                                                       
                                                                                                                                     
event = MISPEvent()                                                                                                                  
event.distribution = 0        # our org only                                                                                         
event.threat_level_id = 2        # medium                                                                                            
event.analysis = 0        # initial                                                                                                  
event.locked = True   # <====== This will allow you to update the event later on
event.info = 'testing new_event'                                                                                                     
event.published = False                                                                                                              
                                                                                                                                     
misp_org = MISPOrganisation()                                                                                                        
misp_org.name = 'bazbaz'                                                                                                             
misp_org.id = 15                                                                                                                     
misp_org.uuid = '5888a98d-a7e8-4183-94bb-4d19950d210f'                                                                               
event.Orgc = misp_org                                                                                                                
                                                                                                                                     
misp = PyMISP(misp_url, misp_key)                                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                     
misp.add_event(event) 
```

Granted, we are testing on a less powerful VM than our production host. We can try testing the two concurrent ingestion PyMISP processes on the production hardware, and it might work. However, we will keep adding more feed ingestions via PyMISP, and at some point, if not now, we will hit the load limit on our production server.

This leads to some initial questions:

1. What is the roadmap for scaling a MISP instance that has reached a load limit?
2. Can you point us to detail suggestions that might help determine the bottleneck? Perhaps focusing on growing that choke point is a temporary solution (e.g. faster disk, more powerful backend db). However, eventually a limit can still be hit.
3. Would it make sense to cache all the various feeds we import into JSON objects that are pending ingestion in a common file set. Then process them serially at some _quiet_ server time so all PyMISP API (or curl) ingestions are basically single threaded? This could be a lot of work as it's counter to our current architecture.
4. What else...?

Not so fast!  First, we have not verified yet that rasterizers have different behaviors in this case.

So, the Autobahn libraries don't use asyncio or Twisted directly -- they go through a "lowest common denominator" sort of library called https://github.com/crossbario/txaio

That means, we want/need to provide an object that wraps over ``txaio.call_later`` and oberstet's idea from above means, basically, this: https://github.com/meejah/txaio/blob/issue66-batched-call-later/txaio/tx.py#L493

So, someone wishing to make "batched" instead of normal ``call_later`` calls would call ``txaio.make_batched_timer`` which will return a thing implementing ``IBatchedTimer`` (having just a ``.call_later`` method). So then any calls you want quantized with particular parameters would call ``.call_later`` on that object, with the same API as the underlying ``txaio.call_later`` (which is itself just a thin wrapper over ``IReactorTime.callLater`` or ``BaseEventLoop.call_later``.

I think the above would still address your use-case (i.e. "I want to set protocol options") but without the need to subclass. As far as implementation, I do like your re-factoring of that code to its own method but I'd keep that as an "internal" helper (i.e. prefixed with an underscore).
IMO, treating the Origin as a triple is pretty much required by the RFC and to make things safe.

Also, just to get a better understanding - how does the protocol get hold of the values from setProtocolOptions?. When I look at the code they are set as attributes of the factory, but from my previous experience with twisted the factory and protocol are separate entities, never the less the options are used from the protocol as attributes of it self?

In this situation, you'd want a `ListField` holding a list of `TextFields`.  You break up the paragraph into sentences yourself, in your `DatasetReader`, make `TextFields` with each of them (using the same `TokenIndexers`), then put them into a `ListField`. def chain_with_contract_setup():

> So user should be able to use them on fallback function like this?
contract.estimateGas().fallback(args, kwargs)?

I think it's okay if you only want to support the newest contract function API, and not the flipped one. So this would be the only one supported:

```py
    contract.fallback.estimateGas()
    contract.fallback.call()
    contract.fallback.transact()
    contract.fallback.buildTransaction()
```

In this ordering, there is no way to collide with the contract's defined function names. You would call a user-defined `fallback` function with: `contract.functions.fallback(...).transact(...)`.

> Also I want to clarify by deprecating contract.constructor().deploy, you mean people should be able to call contract.constructor().transact() without the suffix function name to deploy contract?

It's `contract.deploy()` that would be deprecated. Right, it would be replaced with `contract.constructor().transact()`.

Is there a specific reason why you are determined to do `conda update --all`? I know it may seem intuitive to do so. But in fact, one of the points of the Anaconda distribution is that it provides a collection of packages that have been tested together. Think of it like a "stable" distribution versus the "bleeding edge" that you get with `conda update --all`.

Don't get me wrong---I'm not saying you _can't_ do it. And conda is supposed to work if you do. I just want you to question your assumption that you _should_ do it.

Another alternative is to do `conda update anaconda`. This gives you the latest collection of Anaconda packages. It will look a lot like `conda update --all`, but again, it remains a set of packages tested together.

Finally: when installing additional packages, I would encourage you to use `conda` instead of `pip` whenever possible. Even if we don't have the package in our `default` channel, it's possible that other Anaconda Cloud users have built conda packages for the very packages you are interested in. You can use the command 

`anaconda search -t conda `_\<package\>_

to look for these. I think you'll find options for just about all of the packages you're interested in. If you consider them suitable, you can 

`conda install -c `_\<user\>_` `_\<package\>_

to install them, where _\<user\>_ is the name of the account where the package was found.

Otherwise, yes, the order of operations is good.

> Generally, I prefer procedural approaches with existing objects unless introducing the object adds real mental value that multiple programmers agree on --- and I will be pretty picky about whether it adds value or not.

> To be clear, in conda, I think there are still opportunities to add objects that will have real and defensible mental value (environment objects, for example) for future maintainers.

I feel as though I made a pretty good argument for why having something as fundamental as a package defined as an object is a good thing here.  If you feel my argument is lacking, I would love an opportunity to discuss that.

> However, @ilanschnell fixed the actual issue with a simpler change and so converts this PR into a refactoring of the Package class --- and so it's arguable that the refactoring should be finished so that we don't have two Package objects.

I do want to call attention to the timing of that fix in case it was overlooked.  The commit was done the day after this PR was opened and brought to @ilanschnell's attention.  He fixed it underneath this code without any input, or even notification that it had happened.  If not for that duplication of effort after this was already working, there would not be a fix.  It's hard to address systemic problems through refactoring (in this case, "what constitutes a package?") if band-aids are going to be put on each isolated case as a light is shown on it.

What we need is to be able to specify trusted root certificates to verify the *server*.  In other words, we need to be able to use the option in requests to pass in a path to the verify argument of the `session.get` command.

Maybe it makes sense to just have the `verify_ssl` parameter take a path, and not just a boolean.  That would more cleanly map to the requests api.  Then the patch literally just becomes removing the `bool` call when getting the parameter, and maybe updating documentation?

Thanks for the explanation.  I see the one thing that I haven't explained well and/or just took for obvious.

> Now if someone pip installs conda on a Linux system which uses ucs2, then our Python packages (the ones with C extensions) will not work with their system Python.

That would never happen.  Conda packages would never be in an environment with the system Python.  One of the reasons to enforce everything in an environment is that when you install `numpy` it would download *all* dependencies, including the appropriate version of Python, and install those via conda.  The system Python would only be used to run `conda` which in turn only operates on a conda installation.

It sounds like the USC2/4 issue wouldn't effect us here unless the `conda` package got into a state where it used compiled C code that we somehow shipped with it.  I can't see that happening, so I think we're in the clear.

Hi @amblina, just thought about it a bit more. I think the current behaviour was intentional to ensure consistency for infer_query_length when setting always=True and always=False to avoid the following situation:

```
>>> read.infer_query_length(True)
40
>>> read.infer_query_length(False)
30
```

Maybe there should be an additional option (e.g. include_hard_clipped) that is by default set to False to preserve the current default behaviour?

The reason is that hard-clipped bases are not stored within the BAM.

This options is already available: use the `Libraries` line edit where you can write a list of paths separated by a semi-colon (this is for the -l option as indicated in the tooltip).

There was a bug with the linter and relative copybook paths. The linter actually run cobc on the fly but not on the original source code, instead it creates a copy in /tmp and compile that file in a background process. This screw up relative copy book paths. Now the linter will compile a hidden temp file next to the original source code and place the binary in /tmp (and finally remove that hidden temp file), this should preserve the working directory and fix relative paths.

Hi @gossion 
Actually, the requirements.txt at the root is using for general testing only. [We started to rename these files to dev_requirements.txt](https://github.com/Azure/msrest-for-python/blob/master/dev_requirements.txt). The `azure` meta-package itself does not requires external dependencies. In your situation, you use the [install_requires](https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/blob/master/setup.py#L66) from storage. To be fair, the install_requires of the first real package installed by the `azure`, this will depends on the platform, python version and so on.
That being said, there is no compatibility issue with requests (we still use the same major version number). It's a bug introduced in requests that will be fixed soon for sure. You can freeze, in your application, `requests` in the meantime, [like the Azure CLI did](https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli#install-with-interactive-install-script).
Hope this helps,

I think in the current implementation `GaussianHMM` [only allows|http://hmmlearn.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#gaussianhmm] to specify per-state (not per-state/dimension) prior, therefore `means_weight` must be either a scalar of an array of shape `(n_components, )`.
I just opened an issue noting GMMHMM doesn't work correctly when installed with pip install hmmlearn. Try installing directly from github.

I'm not an expert on the details, but I think what you're looking for is the 'init_params' argument. More info here: https://hmmlearn.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html?highlight=verbose#gmmhmm

It defaults to the string 'stmcw', where the letters refer to
s: startprob
t: transmat
c: covars
w: mixing weights(if you're using GMMHMM, otherwise ignore this value)
(e: emmissionprob for MultinomialHMM)

Taking out a letter takes out the initialization of that variable though. Hope it helps!

`conda` and `pip` are different package managers. `conda` installs the packages from [conda-recipes](https://github.com/conda/conda-recipes) which might or might not be related to Python. `pip` on the other hand deals only with Python packages. `hmmlearn` doesn't have a `conda` recipe, so you must've installed it via `pip`. That is why it is present in `pip freeze` and absent in `conda list`.

I'd say keep it simple and don't try to guess what the developer actually wants when setting any weird timeouts. >multiple instructors on a course

nbgrader does support multiple graders for a single course and there are two ways you would configure this depending on whether you're using JupyterHub or not:

1. Using JupyterHub: see http://nbgrader.readthedocs.io/en/master/configuration/jupyterhub_config.html#example-use-case-one-class-multiple-graders
2. Not using JupyterHub: you will still need access to a shared server, but then you can run the notebook on an open port (using password protection) and give all the graders the link to the notebook server, from which they can access the formgrader.

> one instructor doing multiple courses
> Our initial thought is to add a courses table, and link assignments to that

Can you describe your use case a bit further here? It is not immediately clear to me what the benefit of having a shared database would be... in particular, even if there was support for multiple classes within the same database, you would still need to have a separate course directory for each class (and a separate config file), and separate instances of the formgrader.

> We also want to consider how to move to an external database (postgresql/mysql/etc)

nbgrader does support postgres and likely mysql, so this shouldn't be a problem: http://nbgrader.readthedocs.io/en/master/user_guide/faq.html#do-i-have-to-use-sqlite-for-the-nbgrader-database

There have been some requests to translate in general, and proposal for RTL (see https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/1852). This is a limitation of nbsphinx: it only supports images embedded with `![alt](path)` syntax, not with `` raw html syntax. I'm closing this here and opening it on the nbsphinx repo. In the meantime, it means that if we want images in our docs from markdown cells, we must limit ourselves to the more basic syntax (which precludes custom size hints). So we can use that workaround for now, until nbsphinx adds this feature.

So there's the `IPKernelApp.initialize` method here:
https://github.com/ipython/ipykernel/blob/master/ipykernel/kernelapp.py#L405

This method calls, in this order:
- `init_blackhole(self)`  - might set sys.stdout/stderr to /dev/null according to some settings
- `init_sockets(self)` - eventually sets up the IO loop
- `init_io(self)` - sets sys.stdout/stderr to the captured streams

The easy fix would be to just move the call to `init_io` before `init_sockets`, but I'm not sure what exactly `init_blackhole` is preventing. Maybe those other calls spit out a lot of undesirable output?

Can second that this is about MathJax configuration, namely MathJax being able to render MathML in addition to the currently configured TeX support. In Jupyter's current MathJax configuration, MathJax does not support MathML input at all.

As @rgbkrk noted, kernels wanting to use MathML directly can only do this on Firefox. Most browsers (including Chrome, which had MathML support but killed it some time ago) do not support MathML directly (see the MathML column under https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comparison_of_web_browsers#Web_technology_support) and thus need MathJax to convert MathML to something they can render (e.g. CSS and HTML). Even on Firefox, MathJax may add certain MathML features that Firefox's native MathML renderer does not support (see https://www.mathjax.org/mathjax-2-0-and-the-default-rendering-in-firefox/).

So, in short, Jupyter kernels wanting to output MathML need the change proposed in this PR.

@Yajo guidelines wrt versioning are clear and correct indeed. I don't question that. Note it's not written in the guidelines that each PR must bump `breaking.feature.fix`.

What I say is that the current OCA approach where merge = release is considered harmful. This slows down merges and creates stress and risk. Therefore I advocate progressively evolving towards a process that is more standard in open source projects:

- contributors make PR
- maintainers review and merge them rapidly even if they are not 100% perfect, provided they pass automated quality tests
- maintainers do releases when they deem the code stable

Note it is only a small variation on the existing OCA process.

The best/easiest way we have to signal a release is to bump `breaking.feature.fix`. All I say is that this must be a human decision. If an automated bump is needed it can be done after merge and on a 4th position `breaking.feature.fix.dev`.

`C.__subclasses__()` only returns the immediate subclasses of `C`. The purpose of the registration procedure is to provide a list of *all* descendant classes without resorting to a recursive `__subclasses__()` call on the fly. I'm almost sure that's the only reason `__subclasses__()` wasn't used here in the first place.

So, if you're going to use `__subclasses__()`, it's got to be recursive.

Ah I think I can see the issue. The expression `np.dot(ip, w)` results in an array with shape (60000, 1). The array `op` has shape (60000), so broadcasting means that `np.dot(ip, w) - op` has shape (60000, 60000), which is big.

I think the easiest way to fix this is to make `w1` a 1D array with shape (784,). So
```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad

ip = np.random.randn(60000, 784)
op = np.random.randn(60000)

w1 = np.zeros(784)

def loss(w):
    return np.sum((np.dot(ip, w) - op)**2)

l = grad(loss)
w1 -= l(w1)
```

PS: Glad you like Autograd!

Aha, I appear to have solved my problem. It's another error on my part. log is undefined on nonpositive numbers therefore, det needs to be nonnegative. One way to ensure this is to make X positive semi-definite. So similarly to how you defined `sym`, I defined `psd` as follows

```py
def psd(X):
    epsilon = 1e-4
    n,m = np.shape(X)
    I = np.eye(n)
    return np.dot(X,X.T) + epsilon * I
```

Another interaction to consider is that it's an error for any of these globs/literals to fail to match at least one file. So say I have the following fields:

```yaml
drop: foo.json
pick: *.txt
```

I this case the order of execution actually *does* matter. If drop goes first, the rule will succeed. If pick goes first the rule will fail, because drop will match no files, and the user will see `"foo.json" didn't match any files.` But then we could construct an example with the opposite behavior:

```yaml:
drop: *.json
pick: foo.json
```

That one succeeds if pick goes first but fails if drop goes first. So we have (at least) two different categories of user mistakes we could worry about here:

1. dropping files that are implicitly dropped by `pick`
2. picking files that are explicitly dropped by `drop`

If we're only going to have one of those be an actual error -- since I don't think I want to add special error-discovering code besides the current "empty glob" check -- I think case 2 is much more important to enforce. In case 1 peru you've sort of told peru to do the same thing twice, so your rule isn't very well-formed, but you're going to get the result you wanted anyway. But in case 2 your rule contradicts itself, and you're probably going to get confused. An error would be nice here.

> // being handwavy partial wishlist

> *Every* ID gets:
>
>    - two keypairs generated, a signing keypair, and an encryption keypair.
>    - Optional: the keypairs are generated from a passphrase converted to
>    a long deterministic seed via a KDF so you don't have to manage private key
>    files, but instead enter a passphrase which, through a strong KDF like
>    scrypt or argon2 creates those keys in memory on the fly. Would allow
>    use on multiple machines, impossible to lose private key. Key rotation is
>    trickier though.
>    - the public keys are stored in a known location in the zone file, and
>    marked with a tag which indicates what kind of key it is and how it is
>    serialized. e.g.
>
> {
>     "pubkey_sig": "MHhERUFEQkVFRg==",
>     "pubkey_sig_type": "ed25519",
>     "pubkey_enc": "MHhCRUVGREVBRA=="
>     "pubkey_enc_type": "x25519-xsalsa20-poly1305",
> }
>
> The tags allow software to determine which encryption/signing alg to use
> for the binary key material that is presented. Its important of course that
> implementations in different langs can consume those binary keys directly.
>
> Keys are urlsafe Base64 encoded version of the binary byte array form of
> the key (only 33% larger than the binary form, as opposed to hex which is
> 2x the byte size).
>
> The descriptive tag could also be included by prepending, with a delimiter
> like a colon, to a Base64 encoded version of the binary key. e.g.
> ed25519:MHhERUFEQkVFRg== if you wanted to save the whole thing in a
> single key.
>
> I would not personally choose secp256k1 (aka bitcoin crypto) as the
> primitives for signing unless it is needed for some interoperability. If
> you really want ECDSA perhaps use it with a curve considered safer by the

So what I would suggest is using the asynctest package. asynctest basically adds asyncio support for unittest.

Then your test could look like this:
```
import aiohttp
import asynctest

URL = "http://www.github.com"


class TestOnUnittestClass(asynctest.TestCase):
    def test_get(self):
        req = (yield from aiohttp.request("GET", URL))
        assert req.status == 201
```
and if you run it with pytest, it'd fail.

To me, this seems like a much better and composable way of approaching your issue than adding features to pytest-asyncio pertaining to other testing frameworks.

We must be talking past each other. What do you mean the project file does not exist in the production environment?

restore/build/run/publish all need a csproj file.

If what you are doing is publishing a project from one machine and then copying the published content to another machine and trying to run it, then you don't need to restore and you can't use dotnet run to execute it.

You need one of two things:
1. If the app is a portable application, you need to have at least the shared framework for which the app was published installed in the machine where you want to run it. And you execute it by running dotnet <path_to_your_published_app_dll>. Just be sure that dotnet with the shared framework is in your path or when invoking dotnet give it its full path.
2. If the app is a self-contained app, then, just run your app.exe directly from the published folder.

Notice that in `topicInit()` we require several access to database before we are able to put topic in `hub.topics`:
- https://github.com/tinode/chat/blob/devel/server/hub.go#L288
- https://github.com/tinode/chat/blob/devel/server/hub.go#L307
- https://github.com/tinode/chat/blob/devel/server/hub.go#L387
- https://github.com/tinode/chat/blob/devel/server/hub.go#L453
- https://github.com/tinode/chat/blob/devel/server/hub.go#L576
- https://github.com/tinode/chat/blob/devel/server/hub.go#L597
- https://github.com/tinode/chat/blob/devel/server/hub.go#L597
- https://github.com/tinode/chat/blob/devel/server/hub.go#L719

Yet in every database access there must be latency, right? Especially if we use centralized database like DynamoDB for example.

Yet we only run single instance of `hub` loop which will receive & process all the incoming signal to our system. So in case there are a lot of incoming request for joining new topic, then the system will block until `topicInit()` is done before processing the new request, right?

But if we use goroutine, we could just process new request without waiting call to `topicInit()` finished.

*Pypiserver*, by design, does not perform any requests downstream, And this is required for search to work for missing packages, when you specify `index` and `index-url` in your `pip.conf`.

That is why the [client-side-configurations](https://github.com/pypiserver/pypiserver#client-side-) we recommend to use the `extra-index-url` option.  That way, any missing packages are delegated by *your `pip`, locally* to the central PyPi repo.

If this behavior does not fit your needs, then better use [some other server](https://github.com/pypiserver/pypiserver#similar-projects).

thanks a lot for this great explanation. So sorry for bothering you with this pebkac problem...

I'm really not comfortable adding in new dependencies, particularly in Python 3, but it seems that there is likely a way to implement this ourselves reasonably without the dependency.

My major conceptual concern here is that now we're proactively decoding things to `unicode` that may well be expecting bytes, which may break a lot of stuff. The bytestring is only a problem if you try to decode it, which you know you need to do anyway if you're trying to print strings in Python 2 anyway.

I don't think it's the worst idea in the world, but I'm not convinced that it's adding enough value for the amount of stuff that will likely break if we change the return type of `tzname` (not to mention I think that breaks the `tzinfo` API). I'll have to do a deeper dive and mull this over, but I think the right answer might be "decode your bytestrings properly if you're using Python 2". This is huge part of the reason Python 2 is a total pain.

Collection of fixed problems:

1. Highlighting is using pygments tokens which (much like Vim highlight groups) are to define *what* is highlighted, while powerline defines *how*. Solution: generate names and use custom style.
2. Custom style cannot be registered and used as a regular pygments style because powerline defines only style that can return only highlighting for powerline-specific tokens. Solution: use hacks to alter `TerminalInteractiveShell._style` attribute: one to make changes to style work (that is override of `_make_style_from_name` function) and another to make it work at startup (that is immediate `ip._style` setting in `ConfigurableIpythonPowerline.do_setup`).
3. Powerline renderer was always supposed to output only strings. Solution: that was not much of a problem though, replacing a few occurrences of `''.join` in `powerline.renderer` module did the trick.

Indeed, I realise that we missed out the attribute for alternatives `(a|b)`. I am even more surprised to check on [Try RiveScript Online](https://www.rivescript.com/try) that alternatives is allowed within the optional brackets, such that literally `[a|b]=(a|b|)` where the third option is just nothing or absence of any option. Of course, the `(a|b|)` does not pass the current RiveScript syntax check, but I put that way to illustrate the point and you may consider for possible update. My surprise is probably because you didn't make it clear in the [trigger documentation](https://www.rivescript.com/wd/RiveScript.html#Trigger-Alternations) that alternatives signature `|` is allowed inside optional brackets `[]`, a stronger example would be `what is your [home|cell] phone number?`. This matches exactly 3 cases when replacing the brackets by either `home`,`cell`, or nothing. 

Of course this usage allows flexibility from the perspective of language. But are you afraid that it may cause confusion as well? 

As you have grouped alternatives under the name of optionals, in [brain.py](https://github.com/aichaos/rivescript-python/blob/master/rivescript/brain.py#L444) where `RE.optionals   = re.compile(r'\[(.+?)\]')`, this leads to the ambiguity of `optionals` when sorting is required. Optionals could mean alternatives (parentheses) or non-alternative (square bracket). I may need to add another additional `RE.alternatives` to make clear whether alternatives present.

After training stops, you can resume from any saved point. Choose one of the model snapshots and the corresponding optim-method snapshot to resume (saved in checkpoint path, details see Checkpointing). I've lost the bubble on which methods offer faster calculation.  We have two distinct use cases requiring calculation of full IV curves:
1. ivcurve_npts specified.  In this case, pvlib gets to choose the voltage points.  We are OK with brentq here, or any of the other methods.
2. a vector V (or I) is provided.  In this case I don't see how brentq (or any other method working with the diode voltage) is an option.  Does this leave us with lambertw, bisection and custom newton?  I'm not in favor of custom newton unless it's really an advantage, for the reason that I'd rather focus effort on our application rather than to maintain/improve the guts of a well-known numerical method.

My opinion is to do the obvious: default each case to the method with faster performance, protect the user from having to worry about their details, and keep the interface through singlediode simple.

Indeed, those two need to be separated! 

The normal `=` means "structural equality", i.e., the two things have the same (e.g., bit-vector) representations, but they could be non-equal floating point-numbers. This is the case for NaN, i.e., we have (= NaN NaN) but not (fp.eq NaN NaN). 

Another important thing to remember is that in SMT there is only one NaN, while in real implementations there may be many, i.e., 

    (not (= (_ NaN 8 24) (_ NaN 8 24)))

is unsatisfiable, while in real programs there may be two NaNs that have different (bit-vector) representations.

The `train()` generator I was working on had a flexible interface, allowing for the model and training setup to be specified in a variety of ways and using sensible defaults where possible. I figured I'd give a few examples to get the idea across:

`train(model=model, loss=loss, data=data)`: 
model is a top-level Lasagne layer (or multiple layers), loss is a Theano expression for the loss, everything else is taken care of: parameters are obtained with `get_all_params(trainable=True)`, updates are determined by obtaining the gradient of `loss` w.r.t. the parameters and using a default algorithm (coudl be Adam for example), the structure of the data is assumed to be (X, y) and can be either a pair of numpy arrays, or a generator returning batches. Theano functions are compiled as needed.

`train(model=model, loss=loss, data=data, updates=lasagne.updates.momentum)`: 
as above but the function used to compute updates from gradients is overridden

`train(model=model, loss=loss, data=data, updates=updates_dict)`:
as above, but updates are given directly (so no gradients need to be computed inside `train()`)

`train(model=model, loss=loss, params=params, data=data)`:
as above but now the parameters are not obtained using `get_all_params()` because they are given explicitly (and these are then used to compute the updates).

`train(model=model, loss=loss, data=data, batch_size=batch_size)`: 
batch size is given; is `data` is a pair of numpy arrays, extract batches of this size from them for training. If it is a generator, the generator is now assumed to return "chunks" which the `train()` function further subdivides into batches of size `batch_size`

`train(model=model, loss=loss, data=data, monitor=[monitor_expr])`: 
one or more "monitor" expressions are specified: Theano expressions whose values should be computed by the train function and kept track of.

`train(model=model, loss=loss, data=data, mapping=[input1_expr, input2_expr, ...])`
The data is not in the (X, y) format, so a mapping is specified (in the form of a list of Theano expressions) to specify how the data should be fed into the network.

... and various combinations of the above.


It was maybe a bit too general and I never got around to implementing a working version, but I think the key idea of having very flexible API functions that accept various different combinations of arguments could be very nice from a UX point of view. In 90% of cases, the first invocation (specifying only model, loss and data) is probably sufficient, and this greatly simplifies the code I think. 

So in summary: the `train()` function somehow needs to know about three things: the model, the data and the updates to apply. There are many different ways to specify these three. It would be nice if handling all that boilerplate was abstracted away a bit, by relying on sensible defaults.

Release Notes v2.3.0

Features:

  • add extensions option to DllReferencePlugin
  • add warningsFilter to UglifyJsPlugin to hide warnings selectively
  • add extractComments to UglifyJsPlugin to move kept comments into separate file (i. e. LICENSE file)
  • validate relative and absolute paths in configuration
  • validate stats options
  • allow to match on compiler (name in configuration) in rules
    • i. e. allows to specify different rules for extract-text-webpack-plugin or html-webpack-plugin
  • Allow to pass multiple entries via CLI
  • Performance improvements
  • CommonsChunkPlugin give errors on incorrect options
  • add module.strictExportPresence to make missing export an error instead of warning
  • Compiler emits watch-close event when the watcher is closed
  • Allow additional compress options in UglifyJsPlugin
  • empty chunk numbers are not flags in every chunk, this improves caching invalidation

Bugfixes:

  • OccurrenceOrderPlugin now counts occurrences correctly
  • Fix cheap-source-maps when combined with ES Modules (lines were offset)
  • Watcher now detects file deleting correctly
  • Resolve output path if relative output path is given via CLI
  • Handle stack traces in errors correctly
  • Some usages of System now generate valid code i. e. System.global
  • Dynamic property names are now parsed and can contain webpack stuff (i. e. imports)
  • Empty enviroment variables are now supported in the EnvironmentPlugin
  • (Maybe) Fixes chunk loading in IE when script is cached

Pickling intermediate CFGAccurate results are not supported as of now. However, I believe it is not too hard to add such a support.

The only thing that option ends up modifying is the lifting settings that get passed to PyVEX. It makes it stop IR translation after a single instruction instead of at basic block boundaries.

This means that when you're using `num_inst=1`, it'll take many steps to accomplish the same thing as a single normal step. The results should be identical if you step the two paths in sync (i.e. several num_inst steps for one normal step until they reach the same PC). However, the num_inst path will take more time to execute, obviously.

The only reason you'd want to use num_inst=1 over the default is if you explicitly wanted to get instruction to stop in the middle of a basic block for some reason.

If you're analyzing MIPS code, num_inst=1 will obviously mess things up, since VEX implements delay slots in a way that's incompatible with lifting one instruction at a time.

As a quick note: angr already supports a form of direct taint tracking just by virtue of tracking symbolic variables. I'm not sure that we want to be altering the `name` of the logger objects like this. The `name` should be almost an internal implementation detail of the logger which is independent of what is printed to the screen or file. 

As Ulrik said, for the stdout logger we can probably just not print the name at all and for the file logger, print the whole name:
```python
_formats = {
    'DEBUG': '%(asctime)s "%(filename)s":%(funcName)-10s at %(lineno)d, %(threadName)s: %(levelname)-8s %(message)s',
    'VERBOSE': '%(asctime)s %(name)-35s: %(levelname)-8s %(message)s',
    'NORMAL': '%(levelname)-8s %(message)s',
    'TERSE': 'Ganga: %(levelname)-8s %(message)s'
}
```
should be all we need.

If we really want to print some elided form of the module name in the screen log then a [custom formatter](https://docs.python.org/2.6/library/logging.html#formatter) would be a much cleaner idea.

The message `AttributeError: 'numpy.ndarray' object has no attribute 'items'` is because
 `coeffs[0]` is an array, not a dictionary, so you cannot use `.items()` on it.  The first element of `coeffs` is an array containing the approximation coefficients.  The remaining elements of the list are dictionaries containing the various detail coefficients at each level of decomposition (see the `wavedecn` documentation for more details of the output format).

If you want the approximation coefficients set to zero you can do either:
```Python
coeffs[0] = np.zeros_like(coeffs[0])
```
or 
```Python
coeffs[0][:] = 0
```
For the remaining levels, you can replace the coefficients with arrays of zeros using a statement of the form suggested in #303:
```Python
coeffs[1] = {k: np.zeros_like(v) for k, v in coeffs[1].items()}
```

> We could also support extending the rule for triggering the activation so as to not only be triggered by 403's.

Why did you choose 403 instead of 503 or 429? is it the most common ban code? I think it won't take long until someone propose to extend the http status list, or inspect the body looking for captcha boxes, or "Sorry, no results for you" text in 200 http responses. Users surely need different rules for different websites, so let's add that too.

I'm with @shaneaevans on this, we are going to recreate ban detection logic and expose what took years to collect and build as Crawlera business assets. 

  
> This is not to offer Crawlera to those who don't know it, this is to make it more convenient for Crawlera users to, well, use Crawlera with their spiders.

So the idea is not to help users find out about Crawlera as a solution to their (yet unknown?) banning *or throttling* problems, but to optionally route requests through Crawlera _on first ban_ after they already activated Crawlera to help avoid bans and/or increase crawling rate.

If users already activated Crawlera because of bans, why would they want to use this _enable-on-first-ban_ feature? Once enabled it won't persist across multiple job runs so each job will go through the delay of triggering the ban to enable the middleware.

We enable AutoThrottle extension by default because we want crawlers running in our platform to be polite, but this extension is not compatible with Crawlera, so there are 2 options:

1. Run with AT enabled from the start and turn it off once Crawlera is turned off after first ban.
2. Ignore our politeness policy and run without AT, crawling as fast as possible until it trigger the ban that enables Crawlera MW (and cross fingers it doesnt take website down) 

I don't think option 2 is something we want

Option 1 is a problem too because AT is very conservative about throttling, so it rarely triggers a ban but doesn't provide good request rate either. This is another topic where Crawlera knowledge base about websites excels, Crawlera knows what is the req/s limit a website can sustain. Do we want to maintain a list of throttling limits per website on the extension? no please.

In general I prefer @shaneaevans idea to detect bans by mining information stored in Hubstorage and suggest our users to enable/disable Crawlera from the start per spider.

`Prefetch` objects are a fully [documented feature of Django](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/models/querysets/#prefetch-related). Fundamentally, they allow you to customise the prefetched queryset, so for example you can prefetch only "active" objects rather than all of them. As an explanation of what's going on here, Anaconda needed to un-vendor pip due to concerns about Windows path length limit (260 characters). IIRC, it was the vendoring of vendored deps that was the most painful: https://github.com/pypa/pip/tree/9.0.1/pip/_vendor/requests/packages - this appears to be fixed in master, though, so we won't need to un-vendor future pip versions.

We've been seeing this problem in our environment, too, and this PR apparently fixes it.

Looking a bit into this:

- On the inbound direction (rx msg over serial to NCP) we may need to keep a large enough buffer to store the HDLC output. We process the received data from the callbacks so, it's pretty straightforward.
- On the outbound direction (tx msg to serial from NCP), I think we can treat the buffer as a circular buffer and allow it to contain more than one msg at a time. We may still drop a msg if there is no room left in the buffer to send. 

Also a suggestion about class names and the way they inherit from one another:
-  We have `NcpBase` which implements spinel parsing of the messages.
- And we have `Ncp` which inherits from `NcpBase` and is mainly handling the buffering. 

I think switching the roles may be better, basically making the current `Ncp` the base class, and then have a spinel sub-class (which would implement the current `NcpBase` functionality). With this, we should be able to avoid abstract virtual methods like `OutboundFrameBegin`, ... in the base class.

Any thoughts?

Regarding the label, we are keeping them:

We are not forcing `y` to be numeric.

> t's very inefficient if I want to tokenize all the samples first then apply under-Sampling to create a balanced dataset, rather than undersampling them first then tokenize them.

There is 2 issues:

* I am not personally aware of sampling method which deal with string. If some methods exist and are well know, we will welcome PRs.
* Efficiency is relative. Storing string data is not efficient while the `CountVectorizer` will convert the matrix into a integer matrix which is much more memory efficient and on which we can apply the samplers.

So I think that there is a trade-off there. We need to implement efficient methods working with text extraction but which are recognized. You can comment on #105 if you are aware of any of those methods or even better, you can implement one of those and contribute to the package.

At that point, you're really just fighting the compiler's default naming scheme -- it's also going to generate debug and other support files with the same name but different extensions. (And the debugger is going to expect to find the files like that, so you'll need to adjust it as well.)

The simplest and best thing you can do is to keep the library and Python module in different subdirectories (or parent + subdir). That's the most natural way to resolve name conflicts and you don't need to micromanage the compiler's intermediate and support files. While the support files are safely separated in subdirectories, you can still chose the output path for the main file (`.pyd`) if it's really needed in a specific place (see `LIBRARY_OUTPUT_DIRECTORY` in CMake docs).

So the command to kill your job is `scancel ID`? `sos` just tried to kill all jobs with the job ID, regardless of its existing status, because sos status and pbs status can be out of sync.

Basically tasks are executed and monitored outside of SoS with their own signatures. That is to say,
1. When you submit a task (e.g. to a remote host), you can check its status from another machine, or from Jupyter.
2. Tasks can be translated to be executed on a remote host.
3. Tasks can be executed in parallel (because they are independent)
4. Tasks will continue to execute even if the main sos process is killed.
5. Tasks will not be re-executed if you try to re-run it from a modified or another workflow as long as the tasks themselves are unchanged.

An `inline` task would basically be the old task execution method that has none of the above benefits. It essentially creates a worker pool to execute the task. There would be no signature and no external monitoring, and the tasks will be killed when the main process is killed.

For `R: sigil='${ }'`, the SoS parser would 

1. replace all `{ }` with `{{ }}`.
2. replace `${ }` with `{ }`
3. add `f` to the resulting string.

Basically,

```
R: sigil='${ }'
    if (TRUE) {
        read.csv('${input[0]}')
    }
```
would be parsed to
```
R(f'''
    if (TRUE) {{
        read.csv('{input[0]}')
    }}
```

The issue is about using `f1_score` scorer in multi-class data, please check [this link](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/model_evaluation.html#common-cases-predefined-values). You need specify the right f1 score function name for multi-class data except using `f1`. For example:

```Python
from sklearn.datasets import make_classification
from tpot import TPOTClassifier

X, y = make_classification(n_samples=200, n_features=100,
                           n_informative=20, n_redundant=10,
                           n_classes=3, random_state=42)
tpot = TPOTClassifier(generations=10, population_size=20, verbosity=20, scoring='f1_macro')
tpot.fit(X, y)
```

We put a set of feature selector into TPOT default configuration. TPOT can randomly use those feature selectors (even combine the selected features in different selectors in tree structure) for optimizing accuracy. But so far, we are working/evaluating on a new template function to allow user to specify pipeline structure for feature selection.

I should add: Currently, TPOT uses a *generational* evolutionary algorithm (EA) model. That means that the EA keeps some fixed population of pipelines (by default, 100) and has to evaluate all of them before proceeding to the next generation. From an engineering perspective, generational EAs can be problematic because there can be a single pipeline that takes forever to evaluate and that will hold up the whole process.

For the longest time, we have wanted to re-engineer TPOT to follow a *steady state* EA. With a steady state EA, pipelines are evaluated in parallel and the "parents" of the new pipelines are chosen based on the current population of pipelines that are already evaluated. That way, if one pipeline takes forever to evaluate, the EA can keep on optimizing based on the results from the faster pipelines.

The diagram below sort of communicates those differences, if you stare at it long enough.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1719223/42972661-084d073c-8b65-11e8-9692-c54c68ff2002.png)

Just something to keep in mind as this branch is worked on. I suspect that dask would seamlessly support this huge upgrade to TPOT.

Hi Aaron. The Send OS Command operation at the WS API does not return its output. The output appears in the OS message channel, but the difficulty is correlating the output stream that appears there, with the command that was issued. That difficulty is also present at the interface to the operating system running in the partition. We can have another look at this, but chances are dim that it can be solved.

Your best chance is to combine Send OS Command with the output stream of the OS message channel, and have a human correlate command and output. If you know that you're the only one sending OS commands to a particular partition, then of course you can assume that anything that comes back after sending the command belongs to the command output, but you don't know when the output is complete, nor do you know whether the output contains data originated by something else but your command.

The zhmccli project has implemented a command shell based on sending any input line as an OS command, and displaying the OS message channel in the shell as output. Look at the `zhmc partition console` command, whose relevant function implementing this is [here](https://github.com/zhmcclient/zhmccli/blob/master/zhmccli/_helper.py#L629).

Let's say you have two `TxOut`s, `txout0` and `txout1`, that you want to spend in a new transaction:
```python
solver0 = ...  # solves txout0.script_pubkey
solver1 = ...  # solves txout1.script_pubkey
unsigned_tx = ... # transaction with two inputs, one per txout you want to spend
signed = unsigned_tx.spend([txout0, txout1], [solver0, solver1])
```
of course, if the scriptPubKeys of the two txouts are the same, you can as well use the same solver, but still you'll have to pass it twice:

```python
solver = ...  # solves both txout0.script_pubkey and txout1.script_pubkey
unsigned_tx = ...  # transaction with two inputs, one per txout you want to spend
signed = unsigned_tx.spend([txout0, txout1], [solver, solver])
```

Ughhhh of course it doesn't. Ughhhh.

So... our two options are to either find some way to move back towards normality, so trio-asyncio automatically works because we're actually doing what it expects, or to double down on our elaborate house of mirrors. In general my instinct is to do the former, but in this case that would require going back to nesting fixtures inside each other, and I just can't see how to make that work. So... plan B.

Here's my evil idea: what if we make it so that all the tasks we're creating for fixtures, actually share a contextvar context, so changes one fixture makes are visible to the others. Then when trio-asyncio registers stashes the loop in a contextvar, the subsequent fixtures and test will be able to find it.

The main thing that gives me pause here is that if we literally use the same `Context` object for all the fixtures, then there's a theoretical possibility of interactions and race conditions between otherwise-independent fixtures, if they run in parallel and are messing with the same contextvar. Of course, if you have to fixtures running in parallel and messing with the same contextvar then I can't see how you could ever expect that to work anyway... But there is another option I guess, which is to use separate contexts, but when we start up a new fixture the first thing it does is copy over all the contextvar settings from all the fixtures it depends on. But this requires doing something clever, like a 3-way merge, to detect which contextvars are most up-to-date.

I guess we could literally do a three way merge. Or really go wild and do [mark-merge](http://wiki.monotone.ca/MarkMerge/).

This is hilarious overkill and ridiculous but now that I've said it I'm kind of tempted. I guess it's not even the fully general case, since we would error out on conflicts (trying to depend on two fixtures that set the same contextvar to different values), and this means there would never be resolved conflicts in the history.

I know this is necro, but I figure since we're kicking new life into this, may as well revisit some of these open threads.  

I could be wrong, but my understanding of why this seems to happen is that you'd need to use an async agent like A3C or async DDPG or whatever to make use of all possible threads on your processor. If you're using the cpu version of tensorflow, it should utilize most of your CPU to train the neural networks, but unlike with traditional machine learning, the neural networks only get updated after your environment takes a step, and that training is only for a sample size of 1, so that part can run pretty quickly and may just show up on your CPU load as a quick peak. The environment you're using may be rather CPU intense (like mine has to a ton of math each step), and unless your environment is multi-threading somehow, that's just going to run on one CPU.  A3C and other asynchronous algorithms will create multiple actor/critic pairs that will run in parallel with one another, so with those, you can use your full CPU (and GPU) potential. That's why they're so popular, and why I want to get async DDPG built here.
